verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: What nonsense is this out of which they fabricate a charge against me!

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It seems hardly worth while to notice it.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It is a story of my own about the council held by Damasus Bishop of Rome, and I, under the name of a certain friend of his, am attacked for it.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He had given me some papers about church affairs to get copied; and the story describes a trick practised by the Apollinarians who borrowed one of these, a book of Athanasius’ to read in which occur the words ‘Dominicus homo,’ and falsified it by first scratching out the words, and then writing them in again on the erasure, so that it might appear, not that the book had been falsified by them, but that the words had been added by me.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I beg you, my dearest friend, that in these matters of serious interest to the church, where doctrinal truth is in question, and we are seeking for the authority of our predecessors for the well-being of our souls to put away silly stuff of this kind, and not take mere after-dinner stories as if they were arguments.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: For it is quite possible that, even after you have heard the true story from me, another who does not know it may declare that it is made up, and composed in elegant language by you like a mine of Philistion or a song of Lentulus or Marcellus.
